chunk_summary: This section discusses the use of mid-parental height for assessing short stature, red flags for stunting, and differential diagnosis, emphasizing phenotype-based prioritization and referral triggers for underlying diseases.
learning_objectives: Understand how to calculate mid-parental height and its role in assessing short stature.
Identify red flags and referral triggers for stunting and underlying diseases.
Differentiate between stunting with and without wasting and their clinical implications.
Recognize common treatable causes of stunting in specific populations, such as India.
key_facts: Mid-parental height formula: Boys = (mother’s height + father’s height + 13) / 2; Girls = (mother’s height + father’s height - 13) / 2.
Stunting is defined as HAZ < -2 SD; stunting without wasting (WHZ/WLZ ≥ -2) suggests long-term constraints.
Red flags for referral include progressive height velocity decline, delayed puberty, chronic diarrhea, recurrent infections, and dysmorphic features.
Concurrent wasting and stunting (WaSt phenotype) indicates higher short-term risk and requires urgent evaluation.
Common treatable causes of stunting in India include TB, HIV, celiac disease, hypothyroidism, and genetic syndromes.
reference_quote: Mid-parental height estimates expected genetic potential: for boys, (mother’s height + father’s height + 13) / 2; for girls, (mother’s height + father’s height - 13) / 2. (Source: American Academy of Pediatrics EQIPP clinical guide)
document_key_topics: Stunting red flags and referral criteria
WaSt phenotype and its clinical implications
